coredata.dc:title: A novel fuel based on biocompatible nanoparticles and ethanol-biodiesel blends to improve diesel engines performance and reduce exhaust emissions
coredata.dc:creator: Heidari-Maleni, Aram
Gundoshmian, Tarahom Mesri
Karimi, Behzad
Jahanbakhshi, Ahmad
Ghobadian, Barat
coredata.dc:description: Biodiesel fuel is one of the most important alternative sources that can replace fossil fuels. Biodiesel has many advantages such as high stability, non-toxicity, renewability, high flash point and excellent performance. However, it has disadvantages such as increased NOx emissions, oxidation in air, and incomplete combustion. One of the ways to reduce the negative effects of biodiesel fuel is to use additives such as nanoparticles and catalysts. However, the use of metal oxide and metal nanoparticles poses many environmental hazards. Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) is one of the newest types of quantum dots that have extraordinary features such as biocompatibility, high thermal conductivity, excellent solubility, low production cost and non-toxicity. Given the potential toxicity of metallic nanoparticles to living organisms, biological nanoparticles such as GQD can be used to solve this problem. In the present study, the performance parameters and emissions of a diesel engine by adding GQD to bioethanol (with 2, 4 and 6%, vol) and biodiesel fuel (B10) at different engine speeds (1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm) was evaluated. The results showed that B10 + E4 + GQD60 was selected as the optimum fuel at all engine speeds since it increased engine power and torque and reduced CO and UHC emissions. In comparison with the pure diesel fuel (D100), the optimal fuel (B10 + E4 + GQD60) increased the engine power and torque for 24.59% and 20.02%. The optimum fuel also decreased CO and UHC emissions by 35.60% and 42.05% compared to the pure diesel fuel (D100).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diesel engine
Biodiesel
Graphene Quantum Dot
Performance
Emission
Biocompatible nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) synthesis and analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The steps of biodiesel production from fish waste oil.
Synthesis steps of GQD nanoparticles.

TEM analysis of GQD synthesized nanoparticles.
UV analysis of GQD synthesized nanoparticles.
XRD analysis of GQD synthesized nanoparticles.

Diesel engine torque in different conditions (fuel type and engine speeds).
SFC values at various engine speeds and fuel type.
CO emission values at various engine speeds and fuel type.

UHC emission values at various engine speeds and fuel type.
NOx emission values at various engine speeds and fuel type.

Specs EA 10 Plus energy, radiation source of Al Ka

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical properties of fish oil and biodiesel produced.
Properties of fuels used in this study.

Results of variance analysis of the effect of engine speeds and fuel type on performance and engine exhaust gases.

head.ce:title: A novel fuel based on biocompatible nanoparticles and ethanol-biodiesel blends to improve diesel engines performance and reduce exhaust emissions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Faculty of Agriculture and Natural Resources, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Agriculture and Natural Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Faculty of Agriculture and Natural Resources, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Faculty of Agriculture, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Faculty of Agriculture, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodiesel fuel is one of the most important alternative sources that can replace fossil fuels. Biodiesel has many advantages such as high stability, non-toxicity, renewability, high flash point and excellent performance. However, it has disadvantages such as increased NOx emissions, oxidation in air, and incomplete combustion. One of the ways to reduce the negative effects of biodiesel fuel is to use additives such as nanoparticles and catalysts. However, the use of metal oxide and metal nanoparticles poses many environmental hazards. Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) is one of the newest types of quantum dots that have extraordinary features such as biocompatibility, high thermal conductivity, excellent solubility, low production cost and non-toxicity. Given the potential toxicity of metallic nanoparticles to living organisms, biological nanoparticles such as GQD can be used to solve this problem. In the present study, the performance parameters and emissions of a diesel engine by adding GQD to bioethanol (with 2, 4 and 6%, vol) and biodiesel fuel (B10) at different engine speeds (1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm) was evaluated. The results showed that B10 + E4 + GQD60 was selected as the optimum fuel at all engine speeds since it increased engine power and torque and reduced CO and UHC emissions. In comparison with the pure diesel fuel (D100), the optimal fuel (B10 + E4 + GQD60) increased the engine power and torque for 24.59% and 20.02%. The optimum fuel also decreased CO and UHC emissions by 35.60% and 42.05% compared to the pure diesel fuel (D100).

ce:section.ce:para: The industrialization and mechanization of countries around the world is increasing their demand for fossil fuels day by day. On the other hand, the use of fossil fuels generates a huge amount of greenhouse gases, which will have a detrimental effect on the environment. Decrease in fossil fuel sources as well as increase in energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions have led to the introduction of renewable, sustainable, efficient and cost-effective resources with lower pollutant emissions. One of the most important alternative sources of energy that can replace fossil fuels is biodiesel fuel [1–6].

Biodiesel is a clean and renewable kind of fuel with properties similar to those of diesel fuel. It has many advantages such as being sustainable, non-toxic and renewable and having a high flash point and excellent performance. However, biodiesel fuel has its own disadvantages such as increased NOX, poor atomization and incomplete combustion [7–9]. Methods that can reduce the negative effects of biodiesel fuel include the use of additives and catalysts in this fuel [10–12].

Researchers have reported that the oxygen that exists in the additives can provide the fuel with enough oxygen, leading to more complete combustion of the engine and thus lowering the pollutant emissions from the engine. Recently, researchers have succeeded in reducing greenhouse gas emissions and increasing combustion efficiency by adding nanoparticles to biodiesel fuel [13–15]. Many research studies have been carried out on the use of metallic nanoparticles such as manganese and copper [16], sodium [17], iron [18] and zinc [19] as additives to fuels.

Keskin et al. [20] investigated the effect of MnO2 and MgO metal additives to diesel fuels on the performance of a diesel engine and its emissions. They added 8 and 16 μmol/L of these additives to the diesel fuel for the production of laboratory fuels. Their results showed that both additives improved those properties of diesel fuels such as viscosity, flash point and cloud point. Mirzajanzadeh et al. [17] investigated the impact of a new nanocatalyst on improving performance and reducing exhaust gas emissions. They tested the CeO2/CNT hybrid on B5 and B20 fuels at three levels of 30, 60 and 90 ppm. 90 ppm treatment in B20 fuel was introduced as the optimum fuel and reduced exhaust emissions such as NO, CO, UHC and soot by 18.9%, 38.8%, 71.4% and 26.3%, respectively. Power and torque also rose for 7.81% and 4.91%, respectively.

Despite the extensive research into fuel additives, there are still many problems in this field. In addition, the use of metal oxide and metal nanoparticles poses many environmental hazards. One of the solutions to reduce the negative effects of nanoparticles is the use of biodegradable nanoparticles [21–23]. Quantum dots are one of the nanostructures which have many applications. They are a set of nanoparticles whose dimensions are about 1 to 10 nm in size. The unique optical and electrical properties of these materials provide a good basis for their application in various fields. These materials are nowadays used in the manufacturing of solar cells, quantum lasers, super-capacitors, fuel cells, sensors, quantum computers, etc. Quantum dots also have very high stability and low toxicity compared to other nanoparticles [24,25,21].

Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) is one of the newest types of quantum dots which are very popular today due to their unique properties. GQD is a new generation of carbon nanotubes that are in fact graphene sheets with zero-dimensional lighting. Graphene is a unique semiconductor with a zero band gap. Therefore, in GQD we expect to see many interesting phenomena compared to other quantum dots and any other semiconductor [26–28]. Because of its ability to produce cheap and environmentally friendly materials, GQD has extraordinary characteristics such as biocompatibility, high thermal conductivity, excellent solubility, low production cost, non-toxicity, ease of operation and chemical inactivity. GQD has many applications including its being applied as a biosensor as well as its photovoltaic and bio-imaging performances [29–31].

Given the potential toxic effects of metallic nanoparticles on living organisms, the use of biological nanoparticles such as GQD could significantly reduce the risk. In this study, the effect of adding biocompatible GQD nanoparticles to bioethanol (B10) fuel as well as the performance and emissions of a diesel engine fueled with these materials was investigated. To stabilize the nanoparticles within the biodiesel fuel, bioethanol was used as the intermediate fluid. Also, bioethanol contains 35% oxygen which can reduce the emission of exhaust substances such as soot, NOx and CO. Since GQD is highly stable in liquids, the stability of GQD in biodiesel-bioethanol fuel was investigated.

In this study, transesterification method was used to produce biodiesel from fish waste oil. The fish oil was first refined and then reacted with alcohol in the presence of the catalyst. During this reaction the oil was converted to glycerol or ester, the remaining ester being used as biofuel in this study (Fig. 1 ).

In this study fish waste oil was obtained from Dane Talaee Company, Chabahar, Iran. At the factory, the oil is heated during the production process and passed through a micron filter to remove impurities. Therefore, the prepared oil is free of any impurities or water. Absence of water and impurities is important for proper transesterification reactions. The physical and chemical properties of fish oil are reported in Table 5. Generally, suitable alcohols for the esterification reaction are methanol, ethanol, propanol and butanol. Methanol and ethanol are the most widely used alcohols in the transesterification reaction. Methanol has attracted more attention than other alcohols because of its cheapness and better physiochemical properties. Methanol 99.8% was used for the reaction in this study. Propanol 99.8% was also used as a solvent in titration in order to determine the amount of free fatty acids in the oil. Ethanol 99.8% was used in this study to make biodiesel-ethanol-diesel ternary mix. The physiochemical properties of the above-mentioned alcohols are listed in Table 1 .

In order to carry out the esterification reaction in which the carboxylic acid group (–COOH) of the oil reacts with methanol to produce ester and water, sulfuric acid was used as a catalyst. The acid mentioned is made by the German Company Merck KGaA with the purity of 99.6%. The physiochemical properties of this acid are listed in Table 2 .

Potassium hydroxide is the best catalyst in performing the transesterification reaction and thus it was used as the catalyst for the same purpose in this study. It was also used for titration (determining the amount of free fatty acid in the oil). This material was obtained from Merck Germany with a purity of 99% and its properties are reported in Table 3 . An indicator is used to determine the free fatty acid content of the oil in the titration performed before and after the esterification reaction. Phenolphthalin was used as the indicator in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) synthesis and analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, GQD was synthesized by direct decomposition of citric acid gas through the “bottom-up” method. Then, 2 g of citric acid were placed in a microwave device for 5 min (Fig. 2 A and B). Subsequently, liquid citric acid (orange juice) was added to absolute ethanol (made in Germany) and the mixture was subjected to ultrasound for 10 min using an ultrasonic device (400 W) (Fig. 2C and D). After stabilizing the GQD in ethanol, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis, high-resolution power transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), ultraviolet visible (UV) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were measured. The technical specifications of the equipment used in this study are presented in Table 4
. The analytical results of FTIR, HR-TEM, UV and XRD are also reported in Figs. 3–6
, respectively.

In this study, biodiesel was extracted from refined fish oil using trans-esterification method. The properties of fish oil and biodiesel produced are reported in Table 5. Finally, biodiesel (B10) was mixed with GQD + bioethanol. GQD with the concentration of 60 ppm and bioethanol with 2, 4 and 6 vol% concentrations were combined in B10 fuel (90% Diesel + 10% Biodiesel). Fuel combinations include D100 (pure diesel or control sample), B10, B10 + E2 + GQD60, B10 + E4 + GQD60, and B10 + E6 + GQD60. Table 6 presents the physical properties of the combination of bioethanol + GQD60 in B10 fuel.

In this study, a single-cylinder, air-cooled diesel engine (DICOM model, Italy) was used for conducting the experiments. A dynamometer (MPA-40 model; Iran) was applied to measure engine performance parameters. A pollution meter was used to measure engine exhaust gases with technical specifications given in Table 7 . Finally, the performance parameters and exhaust emissions (power, torque, specific fuel consumption, CO, CO2, UHC and NOx) were measured at different engine speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm at full load. These speeds cover all engine working modes. The mean lubricating oil temperatures were 79, 85 and 90 °C for engine speeds of 1800, 2100, and 2400 rpm, respectively. The engine ran for 10 min to ensure the removal of residues of the previous fuel blends in the fuel line. After that the data acquisition process was started and lasted 3 min. This procedure was followed for all blends. The tests were carefully controlled to ensure their accuracy and no error and all data were transferred to a computer [26,32].

A factorial experiment (3 × 5: engine speeds and fuel type) with a randomized complete design was used to analyze the effect of engine speeds at 3 levels (1800, 2100 and 240 rpm) and fuel type at 5 levels (D100 or control, B10, B10 + E2 + GQD60, B10 + E4 + GQD60 and B10 + E6 + GQD60) each with three replications (45 treatments). The effect of each variable on the performance and engine exhaust gases (i.e. power, torque, SFC, CO, CO2, UHC and NOx) was analyzed. Means were compared using LSD test (p < 5%). Results were statistically analyzed in SAS (version 9.1).

Table 8 presents the results of analysis of variance (ANOVA) of the effect of the main factors (different engine speeds and fuel types) and their interactions on engine performance parameters and exhaust emissions. The results show that the effect of main factors and their interactions on engine performance parameters and exhaust emissions is significant at 1% probability level.

Non-similar letters in the Figs. 7–9
indicate a significant difference at the 1% probability level.

Power is defined as the rate of doing work. In other words, power is the amount of work done per unit of time. The effect of different fuels on braking power at different engine speeds is illustrated in Fig. 7. The results show that by increasing the ratio of bioethanol (by 2, 4 and 6%) to biocompatible GQD nanoparticles (with a 60 ppm concentration) in a mixture with a biodiesel fuel (B10), engine power can be increased.

The mixture of B10 + E2 + GQD60, B10 + E4 + GQD60 and B10 + E6 + GQD60 fuels had the highest power and B10 fuel (90% Diesel + 10% Biodiesel) had the lowest power in all engine speeds. Also, biodiesel fuel (B10) produced a lower engine power compared to pure diesel fuel (D100). In similar studies, researchers reported that biodiesel fuels produce less heat than diesel fuels. Therefore, when using biodiesel fuels, the engine power is reduced. In addition, the amount of heating, lubrication and fuel viscosity have significant effects on engine power [12,32].

According to the results shown in Fig. 7, by increasing the amount of bioethanol (by 2, 4 and 6%) in biocompatible GQD nanoparticles (60 ppm concentration) and in the mixture with biodiesel fuel (B10) the engine power increases. This is indicative of improved combustion and the efficient conversion of fuel energy to useful work. This increase in power can be attributed to the energy produced in the cylinder due to increase in the surface to volume ratio of nanoparticles and increase in the heat transfer coefficient because of the nanoparticles in these fuels. These results are similar to reported results by Hoseini et al. [33], Hoseini et al. [34] and Anbarasu and Karthikeyan [35].

The maximum power produced was equal to 5 kW, which belonged to the fuel with the combination of (B10 + E4 + GQD60) and was obtained at the engine speed of 2400 rpm. The minimum power produced was equal to 3.4 kW which was related to biodiesel fuel (B10) and occurred at the engine speed of 1800 rpm. As the engine speed increased from 1800 to 2400 rpm, the engine power showed an upward trend (Fig. 7). At all the engine speeds (1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm), the maximum power output was obtained for the B10 + E4 + GQD60 fuel, with an average power increase of 24.59% compared to D100 fuel (pure diesel).

Fig. 8 shows changes in engine torque due to the addition of GQD + Bioethanol to biodiesel (B10) fuel. The results show that at all engine speeds, the maximum torque belonged to B10 + E4 + GQD60. On average, the amount of torque after using the B10 + E4 + GQD60 increased by 20.02% and 20.24% compared to diesel (D100) and biodiesel (B10) fuels, respectively. One of the main reasons for the increase in engine power and torque can be the addition of GQD + Bioethanol to the B10 fuel mixture, because increasing the surface area to volume ratio of nanoparticles increases the energy produced inside the cylinder and thus increases engine power and torque. Sufficient oxygen in the mixture of fuels containing nanoparticles and bioethanol also improves engine combustion.

The maximum torque for all fuel samples occurred at 1800 rpm. It can be seen in Fig. 8 that by increasing engine speed from 1800 to 2400 rpm, the engine torque decreases. This drop in torque happens due to incomplete filling of the cylinder during the suction phase at higher speeds (though the valve is fully open). Since there is not enough time for air intake and pressure rise inside the cylinder, combustion pressure decreases, which in turn increases the inertia of the moving parts and reduces engine torque. These results are similar to reported results by Örs et al. [36], Ganesan et al. [37] and Heidari-Maleni et al. [26].

The maximum torque produced was equal to 21.71 Nm, which occurred at the engine speed of 1800 rpm with the B10 + E4 + GQD60 fuel combination. The minimum torque produced was 15.2 Nm, which occurred at the engine speed of 2400 rpm with biodiesel (B10) fuel.

The effect of different fuels on specific fuel consumption at different engine speeds is reported in Fig. 9. The results show that at all engine speeds, the lowest SFC is related to B10 + E2 + GQD60. The average SFC reduction rates for B10 + E2 + GQD60 compared to diesel (D100) and biodiesel (B10) fuels were 23.28% and 25.27%, respectively. By increasing the engine speed from 1800 to 2400 rpm in all fuels, the SFC showed an upward trend. At higher engine speeds, SFC increased because of the low braking power and lower fuel consumption at high speed ranges. Specific fuel consumption is a function of fuel braking power. In other words, whenever the fuel braking power is increased, SFC reduction will occur.

According to the results shown in Fig. 9, adding GQD nanoparticles to diesel-biodiesel fuel reduces the SFC. The reason is that adding GQD nanoparticles to diesel-biodiesel fuels improves combustion and increases engine power, thereby reducing SFC. In similar studies conducted before, researchers reported that decrease in SFC caused by adding nanoparticles to the fuel could be attributed to faster combustion, higher heat release rate, more complete combustion and lower viscosity, which results in increased engine power [26,36,12].

Increasing the proportion of bioethanol (by 2, 4 and 6%) in the fuel mixture increases the SFC content. Bioethanol has lower density and thermal value than diesel fuel. However, biodiesel has higher density but lower thermal value than diesel fuel. Thus, the results of the experiments show that high density cannot compensate for the low thermal value. Therefore, this problem can be solved by increasing the amount of bioethanol in the fuel mixture to produce the same power.

The maximum SFC was equal to 510.26 g/kwh which occurred at the engine speed of 2400 rpm using biodiesel (B10). The minimum SFC was 324.52 g/kwh, which belonged to the engine speed of 1800 rpm and the B10 + E2 + GQD60 fuel mix.

Non-similar letters in the Figs. 10–13
indicate a significant difference at the 1% probability level.

Fig. 10 shows the CO emission from the combustion of different fuels at different engine speeds. As can be seen in Fig. 10, the lowest and highest CO emission levels at all different engine speeds belonged to the B10 + E4 + GQD60 and diesel fuel (D100), respectively. Moreover, the lowest CO emission in all treatments occurred at the engine speed of 1800 rpm and the highest CO emission was for the speed of 2400 rpm.

On average, CO emissions for the B10 + E4 + GQD60 fuel compared to diesel (D100) and biodiesel fuels (B10) decreased by 35.60% and 26.74%, respectively, which can be stated to occur due to the presence of GQD nanoparticles in the fuel.

Carbon monoxide is produced in the engine when there is insufficient oxygen for the combustion cycle and the fuel-air mixture is rich enough for the combustion to take place in the engine. Insufficient oxygen to convert carbon to CO2 causes some fuel to remain unburned and some carbon to turn into CO. In similar studies, researchers reported that the use of nanoparticles and bioethanol mixed with fuels reduces CO emissions due to rapid and complete combustion of the fuel as well as the presence of oxygen in them [26,38,39].

The CO2 emission rates for different fuels at different engine speeds are shown in Fig. 11. The results show that B2 + E4 + GQD60 had the highest CO2 emissions at all engine speeds. CO2 emission increased for B10 + E4 + GQD60 compared to diesel (D100) and biodiesel (B10) fuels by 28.19% and 20.99%, respectively. CO2 in the B10 + E4 + GQD60 fuel reached its maximum at all engine speeds (Fig. 11), while the amount of CO in B10 + E4 + GQD60 was the lowest (Fig. 10). This means that the carbon in the exhaust has been converted to CO2. Moreover, adding GQD + Bioethanol to B10 results in adequate oxygenation of the fuel and more complete combustion resulting in reduced CO emissions. These results are similar to reported results by Heydari-Maleney et al. [12] and Hoseini et al. [39].

CO2 emissions are higher in fuel mixtures (B10 + E2 + GQD60, B10 + E4 + GQD60 and B10 + E6 + GQD60) than in the diesel (D100) and biodiesel (B10) fuels, due to the complete combustion of fuel in the engine. These results indicate the superiority of combining GQD nanoparticles with bioethanol fuel (at 2, 4 and 6%, vol) over using a single biodiesel fuel (B10). In similar previous studies, researchers reported that CO2 gas is produced when the combustion is complete in the engine [26,36,38].

Incomplete combustion is one of the factors behind the production of UHC in internal combustion engines. At low temperatures and loads, the mixture of diluted fuel with the fuel does not burn completely and incomplete combustion occurs. Fig. 12 shows the UHC emission rate from combustion of different fuels at different engine speeds. The results show that at all engine speeds, B2 + E4 + GQD60 had the lowest UHC emission. In B2 + E4 + GQD60 fuel, UHC emission reductions were 42.05% and 28.97%, respectively, compared to D100 and B10 fuels. The high catalytic activity of nanoparticles increases the surface area to volume ratio and thus results in an increase in the energy produced inside the cylinder improving the chemical processes and leading to more complete fuel combustion and reduced emission of pollutants. Researchers have reported in similar research studies that adding nanoparticles to a diesel-biodiesel fuel blend reduced the release of UHC due to rapid and complete combustion of the fuel [26,40–43].

Fig. 13 shows the NOx emission from combustion of different fuels at different engine speeds. The results indicate that B10 + E4 + GQD60 had the lowest NOx emission at all engine speeds compared to the other fuels. It can also be seen that by increasing engine speed, the amount of NOx had an increasing trend for all the fuels; increases occurred with slight differences between engine speeds. In similar studies, researchers reported that nitrogen oxide (NOx) was produced through combustion reactions at high temperatures. The major part of nitrogen oxides were nitric oxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2), which were produced in all combustion processes through the conversion of nitrogen in the fuel structure and also through the conversion of nitrogen in the atmosphere due to reaction conditions (high temperature and high oxygen) [26,40].

In this study, the performance and emission of diesel engine exhaust gases were investigated after adding GQD nanoparticles (60 ppm) to B10 fuel (90% Diesel + 10% Biodiesel). To stabilize the nanoparticles inside the biodiesel fuel, bioethanol with 2, 4, and 6 volume percentages was used as the intermediate fluid. Experiments were performed on a single-cylinder diesel engine at three speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm in full load. The results showed that adding GQD nanoparticles to the fuel increased the power and torque of the engine. On average, compared to diesel fuel (D100), adding GQD nanoparticles to B10 fuel increased engine power and torque by 24.59% and 20.02%, respectively. Addition of GQD nanoparticles to B10 fuel at all engine speeds reduced the SFC compared to B10 and D100 fuels. On average, at all engine speeds, SFC was reduced in mixed fuels (B10 + E2 + GQD60, B10 + E4 + GQD60 and B10 + E6 + GQD60) by 23.28% compared to diesel fuel (D100). In addition, compared to diesel fuel (D100), the amounts of CO and UHC emissions were reduced by adding GQD to the fuel by 35.60% and 42.05%, respectively.
The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

Aram Heidari-Maleni: Project administration, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Tarahom Mesri Gundoshmian: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Behzad Karimi: Methodology, Formal analysis. Ahmad Jahanbakhshi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Barat Ghobadian: Writing - review & editing.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review for key challenges of the development of biodiesel industry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Manaf I S A, Embong N H, Khazaai S N M, Rahim M H A, Yusoff M M, Lee K T, Maniam G P. A review for key challenges of the development of biodiesel industry. Energy Conversion and Management, 2019; 185: 508-517.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation of exhaust emissions from a stationary diesel engine fuelled with biodiesel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mofijur M, Rasul M, Hassan N M S, Uddin M N. Investigation of exhaust emissions from a stationary diesel engine fuelled with biodiesel. Energy Procedia, 2019; 160: 791-797.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impact of modified parameters on diesel engine characteristics using biodiesel: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Goel V, Kumar N, Singh P. Impact of modified parameters on diesel engine characteristics using biodiesel: A review. Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, 2018; 82: 2716-2729.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optimized production and advanced assessment of biodiesel: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hanif M A, Nisar S, Akhtar M N, Nisar N, Rashid N. Optimized production and advanced assessment of biodiesel: A review. International Journal of Energy Research, 2018; 42: 2070-2083.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation and measurement of bioethanol extraction from melon waste (Qassari cultivar)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Salehi R, Taghizadeh-Alisaraei A, Jahanbakhshi A, Shahidi F. Evaluation and measurement of bioethanol extraction from melon waste (Qassari cultivar). Agricultural Engineering International: CIGR Journal, 2018; 20: 127-131.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potentiometric of bioethanol production from cantaloupe waste (Magassi Neishabouri Cultivar)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Salehi R, Taghizadeh-Alisaraei A, Shahidi F, Jahanbakhshi A. Potentiometric of bioethanol production from cantaloupe waste (Magassi Neishabouri Cultivar). Journal of the Saudi Society of Agricultural Sciences, 2020; 19: 51-55. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jssas.2018.05.006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on the engine performance and exhaust emission characteristics of diesel engines fueled with biodiesel blends

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Damanik N, Ong H C, Tong C W, Mahlia T M I, Silitonga A S. A review on the engine performance and exhaust emission characteristics of diesel engines fueled with biodiesel blends. Environmental Science and Pollution Research, 2018; 25: 15307-15325.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of nano-additives in diesel-biodiesel fuel blends: a comprehensive review on stability, engine performance and emission characteristics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Soudagar M E M, Nik-Ghazali N N, Kalam M A, Badruddin I A, Banapurmath N R, Akram N. The effect of nano-additives in diesel-biodiesel fuel blends: A comprehensive review on stability, engine performance and emission characteristics. Energy Conversion and Management, 2018; 178: 146-177.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance improvement and exhaust emissions reduction in biodiesel operated diesel engine through the use of operating parameters and catalytic converter: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ramalingam S, Rajendran S, Ganesan P. Performance improvement and exhaust emissions reduction in biodiesel operated diesel engine through the use of operating parameters and catalytic converter: a review. Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, 2018; 81: 3215-3222.
